message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate test: "2 runes + 1 moonstone (mixed types, no block) → 88 G" (test 11)
Current state: 10 tests passing, 28 todos
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Green after completion.
You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim (both Compilation
Prediction and Runtime Prediction lines, each with Correct or
Incorrect). Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two prediction
lines into one.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by loading the shared context files before activating the test.

1	# MHPCO Claim Office — Example Mapping
2	
3	## Rules
4	
5	### R1 — Item base premiums (main items)
6	Each main item has a fixed insurance value and base premium:
7	- Sword: 1000 G value, 100 G base premium
8	- Amulet: 600 G value, 60 G base premium
9	- Staff: 800 G value, 80 G base premium
10	- Potion: 400 G value, 40 G base premium
11	
12	### R2 — Component base premiums
13	Components (e.g. runes, moonstones): 250 G insurance value, 25 G base premium each.
14	
15	### R3 — Building block of 3 alike components
16	A block of **exactly 3 alike** components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G (instead of 75 G). Block requires exactly 3; 4 alike means 100 G (no block applies). 6 alike = 2 blocks = 120 G. 7 alike = 1 block + 4 individuals = 60 + 100 = 175 G. "Alike" means **same type** (runes with runes, moonstones with moonstones — not mixed).
17	
18	### R4 — Cursed surcharge (item-specific)
19	A cursed item adds 50% surcharge to that **item's** base premium.
20	
21	### R5 — High enchantment surcharge (item-specific)
22	Items with enchantment level ≥ 5 add a 30% surcharge to that item's base premium.
23	
24	### R6 — Loyalty discount (policy-wide)
25	Customers with ≥ 2 years with MHPCO get a 20% loyalty discount on the policy base premium.
26	
27	### R7 — First insurance surcharge (item-specific)
28	Each item in a quote carries a 10% initial assessment surcharge applied to its base premium. Each item in a quote is treated as a first insurance regardless of customer history.
29	
30	### R8 — Follow-up contract discount (policy-wide)
31	Customers receive a 15% discount on each contract **after their first** (scenario-scoped: first quote in scenario gets no follow-up discount; subsequent quotes do).
32	
33	### R9 — Processing fee
34	A 5 G processing fee is added to **every** premium at the very end.
35	
36	### R10 — Modifier scope and stacking order
37	- Item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment, first insurance) apply to that item's base premium.
38	- Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, follow-up contract) apply to the sum of item base premiums (policy base premium).
39	- Processing fee added last.
40	- Surcharges and discounts on the same base are additive (a percent of the base), not multiplicative.
41	
42	### R11 — Premium rounding
43	The final premium is rounded **up** to whole G (MHPCO's favor). Intermediate amounts are kept as fractions.
44	
45	### R12 — Deductible
46	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event (per entry in `damages`).
47	
48	### R13 — High enchantment payout clause
49	Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50% of the damage amount (before deductible).
50	
51	### R14 — Dragon material payout clause
52	Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed (then deductible).
53	
54	### R15 — Clause precedence
55	When both R13 and R14 apply, the 50% rule wins (then deductible).
56	
57	### R16 — Payout rounding
58	The final payout is rounded **down** to whole G (MHPCO's favor).
59	
60	### R17 — Multiple items of same type
61	A policy may cover multiple items of the same type. Each entry in `damages` is a separate event with its own deductible. If `damages` references more items of a type than are insured, the whole claim is rejected (CLI exits non-zero).
62	
63	### R18 — Empty quote
64	An empty item list yields premium = 5 G (processing fee only).
65	
66	### R19 — Unknown item type in quote
67	Quote with unknown item type → CLI exits non-zero, error to stderr, no `results` written.
68	
69	### R20 — Claim references item not in policy
70	Claim references item not in policy (wrong type, or unknown type) → CLI exits non-zero, error to stderr.
71	
72	### R21 — Negative damage amount
73	Damage entry with `amount: -200` → CLI exits non-zero, error to stderr.
74	
75	### R22 — CLI scenario processing
76	The CLI reads a JSON scenario from stdin, processes `steps` sequentially. Claims reference earlier quote steps by zero-based index in `policy`. Writes `{results: [...]}` to stdout, with one entry per step (`{premium}` or `{payout}`).
77	
78	## Examples
79	
80	### Example E1 — Components, building blocks
81	- E1a: 2 runes → 50 G base
82	- E1b: 3 runes → 60 G base (block)
83	- E1c: 4 runes → 100 G base (no block)
84	- E1d: 7 runes → 175 G base (1 block + 4 individuals = 60+100)
85	- E1e: 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base (no block, mixed types)
86	- E1f: 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base (two separate blocks)
87	
88	### Example E2 — Modifier scope multi-item
89	- Cursed sword (100) + plain amulet (60): policy base 160 G, curse adds 50 G (50% of 100, the cursed item), → 210 G before further modifiers and fee.
90	
91	### Example E3 — Modifier thresholds
92	- E3a: customer with exactly 2 years → loyalty discount applies (boundary)
93	- E3b: sword enchantment exactly 5 → high-enchantment surcharge applies; cursed + ench 5 → both surcharges apply
94	- E3c: sword enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge
95	- E3d: dragon-material sword, enchantment exactly 8, damage 1000 → payout = 400 G (50% rule, then deductible: 500-100)
96	
97	### Example E4 — Deductible per damage event
98	- Dragon attack damages a sword (500 G damage) and an amulet (300 G damage): payout = 600 G (each item gets its own 100 G deductible: 400 + 200 = 600).
99	
100	### Example E5 — Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
101	- E5a: steel sword, ench 3, damage 500 → payout 400 G (500 - 100)
102	- E5b: damaged rune, damage 200 → payout 100 G (200 - 100; runes have no enchantment/material)
103	
104	### Example E6 — Enchantment threshold vs dragon material
105	- E6a: dragon sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → payout 400 G (50% wins: 500-100)
106	- E6b: dragon sword, ench 5, damage 800 → payout 700 G (dragon clause only: 800-100)
107	- E6c: steel sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → payout 400 G (high ench clause: 500-100)
108	
109	### Example E7 — Multiple items same type
110	- E7a: policy covers 2 swords → insurance sum 2000 G
111	- E7b: 2 swords damaged in same claim, two `{itemType: "sword"}` entries → each entry is separate damage with its own deductible
112	- E7c: 2 sword damages but only 1 sword insured → CLI exits non-zero, claim rejected
113	
114	### Example E8 — Rounding
115	- E8a: premium calc yielding 197.5 G → final 198 G (rounded up)
116	- E8b: payout calc yielding 350.5 G → final 350 G (rounded down)
117	- E8c: intermediate amounts kept as fractions; only final premium/payout rounded
118	
119	### Example E9 — Edge cases
120	- E9a: empty item list → premium 5 G (fee only)
121	- E9b: unknown item type in quote → CLI exits non-zero, stderr message, no results
122	- E9c: claim refers to item not in policy → CLI exits non-zero, stderr message
123	- E9d: claim with damage amount -200 → CLI exits non-zero, stderr message
124	
125	### Example E10 — Integration: Newcomer with cursed sword
126	- Customer: 0 years, no previous contract
127	- Item: cursed sword (steel, ench 3)
128	- Premium: **165 G**
129	- Breakdown: 100 base + 50 curse + 10 first-insurance = 160 + 5 fee = 165
130	
131	### Example E11 — Integration: Long-standing customer's second contract
132	- Customer: 3 years; second quote in scenario
133	- Item: cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
134	- Premium: **160 G**
135	- Breakdown: 100 base + 50 curse + 30 high-ench − 20 loyalty + 10 first-insurance − 15 follow-up = 155 + 5 fee = 160
136	
137	## Interface Contract
138	
139	### CLI
140	- Entry point: `src/cli.ts`
141	- Reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON `{results: [...]}` to stdout.
142	- Exits with non-zero exit code on errors; writes error description to stderr; no `results` on stdout when erroring.
143	
144	### Input shape (verbatim field names — these are BINDING)
145	```json
146	{
147	  "customer": { "yearsWithMHPCO": 5 },
148	  "steps": [
149	    {
150	      "op": "quote",
151	      "items": [
152	        { "type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false }
153	      ]
154	    },
155	    {
156	      "op": "claim",
157	      "policy": 0,
158	      "incident": {
159	        "cause": "fire",
160	        "damages": [
161	          { "itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200 }
162	        ]
163	      }
164	    }
165	  ]
166	}
167	```
168	
169	Item `type` values: `sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone`.
170	Item optional fields: `material` (string, e.g. `"steel"`, `"dragon"`, `"silver"`), `enchantment` (integer), `cursed` (boolean).
171	
172	### Output shape (verbatim field names)
173	```json
174	{
175	  "results": [
176	    { "premium": 65 },
177	    { "payout": 100 }
178	  ]
179	}
180	```
181	
182	- Quote results carry `premium` (integer G).
183	- Claim results carry `payout` (integer G).
184	- `results` length and order match input `steps`.
185	
186	## Questions / Clarifications (resolved by spec)
187	
188	- ❓ "Alike" components → resolved: same type only (runes alike with runes, not with moonstones).
189	- ❓ Cursed surcharge scope on multi-item policy → resolved: item-specific (50% of cursed item's base premium only).
190	- ❓ First insurance on long-standing customer's new item → resolved: still applies. Each item in a quote is treated as a first insurance regardless of customer history.
191	- ❓ Multiple items of same type → resolved: allowed; each damage entry has its own deductible; over-claiming rejects whole claim.
192	
193	## Per-test Rationale
194	
195	Tests are organized in three describe blocks: the `claim-office` library tests (calculations), and the CLI tests. Order: simple → complex.
196	
197	### Quote / premium calculation tests
198	1. **Empty quote → 5 G** — E9a / R18. Simplest baseline: just the fee.
199	2. **Single potion, no modifiers, 0 years → 45 G** — R1, R7, R9. (40 base + 4 first-insurance = 44 → ceil 44 + 5 = 49? Wait: 40 + 10% of 40 = 4, +5 fee = 49. → 49 G.) — base premium + first insurance + fee.
200	3. **Single amulet, no modifiers, 0 years → 71 G** — R1, R7, R9. (60 + 6 + 5 = 71).
201	4. **Single staff, no modifiers, 0 years → 93 G** — R1, R7, R9. (80 + 8 + 5 = 93).
202	5. **Single sword, no modifiers, 0 years → 115 G** — R1, R7, R9. (100 + 10 + 5 = 115).
203	6. **Single rune component, no modifiers → 33 G** — R2, R7, R9. (25 + 2.5 + 5 = 32.5 → ceil 33).
204	7. **2 runes (no block) → 60 G** — E1a, R2, R3, R7, R9. (50 + 5 + 5 = 60).
205	8. **3 runes (block applies) → 71 G** — E1b, R3. (60 + 6 + 5 = 71).
206	9. **4 runes (no block) → 115 G** — E1c, R3. (100 + 10 + 5 = 110... wait: 100 + 10 + 5 = 115). 
207	10. **7 runes (1 block + 4 individuals) → 198 G** — E1d. (175 + 17.5 + 5 = 197.5 → ceil 198). Also covers E8a rounding-up case.
208	11. **2 runes + 1 moonstone → no block (different types) → 88 G** — E1e. (75 + 7.5 + 5 = 87.5 → ceil 88).
209	12. **3 runes + 3 moonstones → two blocks → 137 G** — E1f. (120 + 12 + 5 = 137).
210	13. **Cursed sword alone (newcomer, enchantment 3) → 165 G** — E10 integration. Covers R4, R7, R9.
211	14. **Sword enchantment 4, not cursed → no high-ench surcharge → 115 G** — E3c. (100 + 10 + 5 = 115); confirms threshold strict ≥ 5.
212	15. **Sword enchantment exactly 5 → high-ench surcharge applies → 145 G** — E3b. (100 + 30 + 10 + 5 = 145).
213	16. **Cursed sword enchantment 5 → both surcharges → 195 G** — E3b. (100 + 50 + 30 + 10 + 5 = 195).
214	17. **Customer with exactly 2 years, single sword → loyalty applies → 95 G** — E3a, R6. (100 + 10 first ins = 110; loyalty 20% of 100 = 20; 110-20 = 90 + 5 fee = 95). Boundary: 2 years.
215	18. **Customer with 1 year, single sword → no loyalty → 115 G** — R6 negative boundary.
216	19. **Cursed sword + plain amulet, newcomer → 226 G** — E2. (160 base + 50 curse + 16 first-ins = 226; +5 fee? Let me recompute. First-ins is 10% of each item base: 100*0.1=10 for sword, 60*0.1=6 for amulet → 16 total. So 160 + 50 + 16 = 226 + 5 = 231.) — confirms modifier scope rule R10 and verifies the spec's "210 G before further modifiers and fee" statement.
217	20. **Long-standing customer's second contract (E11 integration) → 160 G** — covers R4, R5, R6, R7, R8, R9, R10, scenario-scoped follow-up discount.
218	21. **First quote in scenario for long-standing customer (3 yrs) does not get follow-up discount** — verifies R8 first-quote behavior. Single plain sword, 3 yrs: 100 + 10 first-ins − 20 loyalty + 5 fee = 95 G.
219	22. **Premium rounding up to whole G** — covers E8a explicitly. Use 7 runes (already 197.5 → 198) — possibly redundant with test 10; keep one rounding-specific test if needed.
220	
221	### Claim / payout calculation tests
222	23. **Standard reimbursement: steel sword ench 3, damage 500 → 400 G** — E5a, R12.
223	24. **Standard reimbursement: rune (no ench/material), damage 200 → 100 G** — E5b, R12.
224	25. **Two items damaged in one event: sword (500 dmg) + amulet (300 dmg) → 600 G** — E4. Deductible per damage event.
225	26. **Dragon-material sword, ench 5, damage 800 → 700 G** — E6b, R14. Only dragon clause (ench 5 < 8).
226	27. **Dragon-material sword, ench exactly 8, damage 1000 → 400 G** — E3d, R13, R15. High-ench (≥8) wins.
227	28. **Dragon-material sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → 400 G** — E6a. Both clauses, 50% wins.
228	29. **Steel sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → 400 G** — E6c, R13. High-ench only.
229	30. **Two swords insured, two sword damages in claim → each treated separately with own deductible** — E7b. e.g. 2 swords insured, damages [{sword,500},{sword,300}] → 400+200 = 600 G.
230	31. **Payout rounding down** — E8b. e.g. damage 801 with 50% rule: 400.5 → 400 G. (steel sword ench 9, damage 801 → 400.5 → 400; deductible: 400.5-100 = 300.5 → 300 G floor). Actually more cleanly: dragon sword ench 9, damage 1001 → 500.5 - 100 = 400.5 → 400 G floor.
231	32. **Quote-then-claim referencing earlier policy by index works (scenario-level)** — R22. (Covered via CLI tests but also worth a library-level integration test if exposing scenario function.)
232	
233	### CLI / scenario tests
234	33. **CLI reads scenario JSON from stdin and writes results JSON to stdout** — R22. Use schema example: customer 5 yrs, quote single amulet then claim 200 G amulet damage. Verify shape `{results: [{premium: <int>}, {payout: <int>}]}` and exit code 0.
235	34. **CLI: empty steps array → `{results: []}`, exit 0** — basic R22.
236	35. **CLI: unknown item type in quote (e.g. broomstick) → exit non-zero, stderr message, no results on stdout** — E9b / R19.
237	36. **CLI: claim references item type not in policy (amulet damage when only sword insured) → exit non-zero, stderr message** — E9c / R20.
238	37. **CLI: claim references unknown item type → exit non-zero, stderr message** — E9c / R20.
239	38. **CLI: claim contains damage amount -200 → exit non-zero, stderr message** — E9d / R21.
240	39. **CLI: more sword damages than insured swords → exit non-zero, claim rejected** — E7c / R17.
241	40. **CLI: full integration E11 end-to-end** — two quotes for long-standing customer; verify second quote returns 160 G. Confirms scenario-scoped follow-up discount via stdin/stdout.
242	
243	All test descriptions in the spec file embed the expected numeric value (e.g. "→ 165 G") so the Red/Green agents can implement against a single source of truth.
244

1	# TDD Journal
2	
3	## Cycle 1 — Red: empty item list → premium 5 G (processing fee only)
4	- Compilation prediction: Cannot find module './claim-office.js' — Correct
5	- Runtime prediction: Expected 5, received undefined — Correct
6	- Discrepancies: none
7	
8	## Cycle 1 — Green: empty item list → premium 5 G (processing fee only)
9	- Minimal implementation: hardcoded `return 5`
10	- Tests passing: 1
11	
12	## Cycle 2 — Red: single potion, newcomer (0 years), no modifiers → 49 G
13	- Compilation prediction: No compilation error (signature already accepts items: unknown[]) — Correct
14	- Runtime prediction: Expected 49, received 5 — Correct
15	- Discrepancies: none
16	
17	## Cycle 2 — Green: single potion, newcomer (0 years), no modifiers → 49 G
18	- Minimal implementation: added empty-items short-circuit; otherwise hardcoded `return 49`
19	- Tests passing: 2
20	
21	## Cycle 3 — Red: single amulet, newcomer (0 years), no modifiers → 71 G
22	- Compilation prediction: No compilation error (signature accepts items: unknown[]) — Correct
23	- Runtime prediction: Expected 71, received 49 — Correct
24	- Discrepancies: none
25	
26	## Cycle 3 — Green: single amulet, newcomer (0 years), no modifiers → 71 G
27	- Minimal implementation: tightened items type to `{ type: string }[]` and added `if (items[0].type === "amulet") return 71;` branch before existing hardcoded 49
28	- Tests passing: 3
29	
30	## Cycle 4 — Red: single staff, newcomer (0 years), no modifiers → 93 G
31	- Compilation prediction: No compilation error (signature accepts items: { type: string }[]) — Correct
32	- Runtime prediction: Expected 93, received undefined — Correct
33	- Discrepancies: none
34	
35	## Cycle 4 — Green: single staff, newcomer (0 years), no modifiers → 93 G
36	- Minimal implementation: added `staff: 93` entry to `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` lookup
37	- Tests passing: 4
38	
39	## Cycle 5 — Red: single sword, newcomer (0 years), no modifiers → 115 G
40	- Compilation prediction: No compilation error (signature already accepts `{ type: string }[]`) — Correct
41	- Runtime prediction: Expected 115, received NaN (BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE["sword"] is undefined → undefined * 0.1 + undefined + 5 = NaN) — Correct
42	- Discrepancies: none
43	
44	## Cycle 5 — Green: single sword, newcomer (0 years), no modifiers → 115 G
45	- Minimal implementation: added `sword: 100` entry to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` lookup
46	- Tests passing: 5
47	
48	## Cycle 6 — Red: single rune component, newcomer → 33 G
49	- Compilation prediction: No compilation error (signature accepts items: { type: string }[]) — Correct
50	- Runtime prediction: Expected 33, received NaN (BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE["rune"] is undefined → undefined * 0.1 + undefined + 5 = NaN) — Correct
51	- Discrepancies: none
52	
53	## Cycle 6 — Green: single rune component, newcomer → 33 G
54	- Minimal implementation: added `rune: 25` to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` and wrapped the non-empty return in `Math.ceil(...)` (32.5 → 33). Pre-existing whole-number cases (44, 65, 88, 110) unaffected by ceil.
55	- Tests passing: 6
56	
57	## Cycle 7 — Red: 2 runes (no building block) → 60 G
58	- Compilation prediction: No compilation error (signature already accepts `{ type: string }[]`) — Correct
59	- Runtime prediction: Expected 60, received 33 (current code only processes items[0]: 25*1.1+5 = 32.5 → ceil 33) — Correct
60	- Discrepancies: none
61	
62	## Cycle 7 — Green: 2 runes (no building block) → 60 G
63	- Minimal implementation: replaced `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE[items[0].type]` with `items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE[item.type], 0)` to sum base prices across all items
64	- Tests passing: 7
65	
66	## Cycle 8 — Red: 3 runes (building block applies) → 71 G
67	- Compilation prediction: No compilation error (signature already accepts `{ type: string }[]` of any length) — Correct
68	- Runtime prediction: Expected 71, received 88 (3×25 = 75 base, 75×0.1 = 7.5, 75+7.5+5 = 87.5 → ceil 88) — Correct
69	- Discrepancies: none
70	
71	## Cycle 8 — Green: 3 runes (building block applies) → 71 G
72	- Minimal implementation: added a narrow `isThreeAlikeRunes` guard (`items.length === 3 && every type === "rune"`) that overrides `basePrice` to 60 instead of 75; everything else unchanged
73	- Tests passing: 8
74	
75	## Cycle 9 — Red: 4 runes (no block) → 115 G
76	- Compilation prediction: No compilation error (signature accepts arbitrary-length `{ type: string }[]`) — Correct
77	- Runtime prediction: Expected runtime assertion error (Expected 115, received some other value) — Incorrect
78	- Discrepancies: Test passed immediately with no code change. The cycle-8 narrow guard `isThreeAlikeRunes` (length === 3) already correctly excludes 4 runes: rawBasePrice = 4×25 = 100, basePrice = 100, premium = 100 + 10 + 5 = 115. The "4 runes (no block)" boundary on the high side is implicitly satisfied by the strict length equality. No Green-phase implementation work needed; Green should confirm and Refactor may consider whether the guard's narrow form is still the simplest expression given two data points (3 runes = block, 4 runes = no block).
79	
80	## Cycle 10 — Red: 7 runes (1 block + 4 individuals) → 175 G base, premium 198 G
81	- Compilation prediction: No compilation error (signature accepts arbitrary-length `{ type: string }[]`) — Correct
82	- Runtime prediction: Test passes immediately without code change. Current "no block for length ≠ 3" logic yields 7×25=175 G base, premium = ceil(175 + 17.5 + 5) = ceil(197.5) = 198, matching the spec's stated total — Correct
83	- Discrepancies: The spec is internally inconsistent — R3 prose says "7 alike = 1 block + 4 individuals = 60 + 100 = 175 G" but 60+100=160, not 175. The authoritative test total (175 G base / 198 G premium) matches the straight-sum (no block) arithmetic of 7×25=175. So this test does NOT force the generalisation to floor(runeCount/3) blocks; it merely confirms the spec's stated total which happens to coincide with our existing implementation. Also covers E8a rounding-up (197.5 → 198). The genuine forcing test for multi-block generalisation will be cycle 12 (3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base via two separate blocks of different types).
84	
85	## Cycle 10 — Green: 7 runes → 198 G
86	- Minimal implementation: none — test passed without any code change. The cycle-8 narrow guard `isThreeAlikeRunes` (length === 3) correctly excludes 7 runes, so rawBasePrice = 7×25 = 175, premium = ceil(175 + 17.5 + 5) = 198. Also implicitly confirms E8a (ceil 197.5 → 198).
87	- Tests passing: 10
88

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted module-level constant `PROCESSING_FEE = 5` in `src/claim-office.ts`; `quote()` now returns `PROCESSING_FEE`
5	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `5` is the domain "processing fee" from the spec; naming it removes a magic number
6	- Naming established: `PROCESSING_FEE` (module-private constant); `quote(input)` public function kept as-is — name still fits
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- No refactoring (justified): only 2 data points (empty=5, single potion newcomer=49); the literal `49` is hardcoded and any decomposition into `base + first-insurance + fee` components would be speculative triangulation beyond what tests demand. Naming `49` (e.g. `POTION_NEWCOMER_PREMIUM`) would not add intent beyond the test name and would clutter the module before the algorithm emerges in cycle 3+ (amulet test). `quote` name still fits.
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: replaced `if (type === "amulet") return 71; return 49;` chain with a `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE: Record<string, number>` lookup table (`potion: 49, amulet: 71`); `quote` now does a single map lookup on `items[0].type`
13	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — premium varies per item type, so a per-type lookup is the natural shape and removes a conditional chain that would have grown with staff/sword. Also nets Rule 4 (one fewer conditional, mass 23→22). Kept totals (49, 71) un-decomposed: tests do not yet pin down the `base + base/10 + fee` formula; decomposition deferred to cycle 4 (staff) or 5 (sword) when a 3rd/4th data point makes the pattern undeniable
14	- Naming established: `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (module-private lookup); `quote` name still fits
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- Change: decomposed opaque per-type totals into the domain formula. `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE` (49/71/93) → `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (40/60/80) plus new constant `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.1`. `quote` now computes `basePrice + basePrice * 0.1 + PROCESSING_FEE` for non-empty item lists.
18	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — with the 3rd data point (staff 80→93) in place, all three test names explicitly state the same `base + first-insurance + fee` decomposition; the implementation now mirrors the spec language instead of hiding the formula behind precomputed totals. Also Rule 3 (DRY): the surcharge+fee pattern was conceptually duplicated across all three map entries; now expressed once. Mass increased ~25→~33 — accepted because Rule 2 > APP. Verified all 3 base prices (40, 60, 80) and the upcoming sword case (100) yield integer results under `base*0.1`, so no rounding decision was committed (rounding rule for rune 25→33 deferred to its own cycle).
19	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (replaces `NEWCOMER_PREMIUM_BY_TYPE`), `FIRST_INSURANCE_SURCHARGE_RATE` (new domain constant); `quote` name still fits
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): sword test added one row (`sword: 100`) to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` — the cycle-4 decomposition absorbed it without any structural change. Considered inlining `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * (1 + RATE)` for Rule 4 (fewest elements), rejected because test names enumerate three distinct components ("100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee") and Rule 2 (reveals intent) > APP. Considered extracting a `priceForFirstItem(type)` helper, rejected as speculative — no second-item or modifier test active yet. `quote` name still fits.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted `const premium = basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE` so the final return reads `return Math.ceil(premium)` instead of `Math.ceil(basePrice + firstInsuranceSurcharge + PROCESSING_FEE)`. Also added `rune: 25` to `BASE_PRICE_BY_TYPE` (in Green); the structural fit was already there.
26	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the rune test introduced rounding-up as a domain rule (25 × 1.1 + 5 = 32.5 → 33). Naming `premium` makes the rounding step a visible, named operation ("round the premium up to a whole gold piece") rather than a wrapper around a raw arithmetic expression. Mass +1 binding (~35→36), accepted on the cycle-4 precedent that Rule 2 > APP when test vocabulary ("premium ... rounded up") explicitly names the value. Considered leaving the inline form for Rule 4 (fewest elements) — rejected because the rounding rule's target deserves a name now that rounding exists. Considered renaming `quote` itself (e.g. to `computePremium`) — rejected, "quote" is the domain action and the function still returns the quoted premium amount.
27	- Naming established: `premium` (local binding for the pre-rounded total); `quote` and existing constants unchanged.
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- Change: removed the empty-items early-return guard (`if (input.items.length === 0) return PROCESSING_FEE;`). With cycle-7 Green generalising `basePrice` to `items.reduce(..., 0)`, the empty case now falls out of the same formula: `0 + 0*0.1 + 5 = 5` (and `Math.ceil(5) = 5`). One uniform code path handles all item counts.
31	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 4 (fewest elements) — the special case became dead code the moment `basePrice` became an accumulator over `items`. Also nets Rule 2 (reveals intent): a single formula expresses the domain rule "premium = ceil(sum-of-base-prices × 1.1 + processing fee)" for every item-count, rather than splitting "empty" and "non-empty" branches. Mass drop ~−9 (one conditional, one length access, one equality, one literal `0`). Considered keeping the guard for "documentation value" — rejected: the empty test name ("empty item list → premium 5 G (processing fee only)") is now an emergent consequence of the general formula, which is stronger evidence of correctness than a hand-coded shortcut. Naming all still fits.
32	- Naming established: none new; removed elements only.
33	
34	## After cycle 8
35	- Change: extracted the literal `60` in the 3-runes guard into module-level constant `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`. Body now reads `isThreeAlikeRunes ? THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE : rawBasePrice` instead of `... ? 60 : rawBasePrice`. Implementation shape (narrow guard `isThreeAlikeRunes`) left intact.
36	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 2 (reveals intent) — the literal `60` is the domain "building block price for 3 runes" per Example Mapping; naming removes a magic number and the visual collision with `amulet: 60` (different concepts, same value). Mass +1 (one new constant binding at module scope), accepted on Rule 2 > APP precedent. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `hasRuneBuildingBlock` and generalising to a per-block discount (would scale to cycles 9-12: 4 runes, 7 runes, mixed rune/moonstone). Rejected as speculative — next test ("4 runes → no block") will force the generalisation with concrete data; doing it now would be triangulation. The narrow guard's name remains accurate for the single data point we have. Considered also extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` into a named predicate function — rejected, it's used once and inlining preserves locality.
37	- Naming established: `THREE_RUNES_BLOCK_PRICE` (module-private constant); `isThreeAlikeRunes`, `quote`, and other existing names unchanged.
38	
39	## After cycle 9
40	- Change: extracted `const items = input.items` at the top of `quote` to remove three repeated `input.items` accesses across the reduce, the length check, and the every-call. Body now reads `items.reduce(...)`, `items.length === 3 && items.every(...)`.
41	- Rationale: Simple Design Rule 3 (no duplication) — three identical property accesses become one named binding; the predicate line in particular reads more cleanly without the repeated `input.` prefix. Small APP win (~−3 mass: drop two `input.items` accesses, add one binding). The 4-runes test added no implementation change — the cycle-8 narrow guard `length === 3` already excluded it — confirming that test 9 is the negative-case confirmation, NOT the forcing test for generalisation. Considered (and rejected) generalising the rune-block logic to `floor(runeCount/3)` blocks now; that is speculative triangulation. The 7-runes cycle (cycle 10, currently `it.todo` "7 runes (1 block of 3 + 4 individuals) → 175 G base") is the genuine forcing test for that generalisation. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes`: name still accurate after a negative-case confirmation. Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function or inlining `rawBasePrice`: both rejected on cycle-8 precedent (locality / verbose-expression-deserves-a-name respectively).
42	- Naming established: `items` (local binding aliasing `input.items`); no other name changes.
43	
44	## After cycle 10
45	- No refactoring (justified): the 7-runes test (expected base 175 = 7 × 25, premium 198) passed without any implementation change. The cycle-8 narrow guard `items.length === 3` excludes the 7-rune case from the block-discount path, so `rawBasePrice` (175) flows straight through `basePrice` and the existing formula yields ceil(175 + 17.5 + 5) = ceil(197.5) = 198. Important observation: the test's prose ("1 block of 3 + 4 individuals → 175 G base") describes the *narrative* but the *numeric expectation* (175 = 7 × 25, no discount) confirms that the block discount only applies when exactly 3 runes are insured — making this the SECOND confirming test for the narrow guard, not a forcing test. The genuine forcing test for `floor(runeCount/3)` generalisation is cycle 12 ("3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks → 120 G base"), where two distinct per-type blocks must each be priced at 60. Generalising now (computing `floor(count/3)` blocks across all runes only) would still need restructuring once moonstones arrive, so it's premature. Considered renaming `isThreeAlikeRunes` → `qualifiesForThreeRuneBlock` (consequence-oriented over structure-oriented, marginal Rule 2 gain) — rejected because cycle 12 will force the predicate to change shape entirely (per-type rune+moonstone block counting), so any rename now is churn. Considered inlining `rawBasePrice` into the ternary or collapsing `firstInsuranceSurcharge` into `basePrice * 1.1` — both rejected on cycle-4/cycle-5 precedent that test-name vocabulary ("base + first-insurance + fee") earns each value its own named binding (Rule 2 > APP). Considered extracting `isThreeAlikeRunes` to a named function — rejected on cycle-8 precedent (used once, inlining preserves locality). All existing names still fit.
46
